coredata.dc:title: Corrosion inhibition behavior and mechanism of N-doped carbon dots for metal in acid environment
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhixiong
Ye, Y.W.
Chen, H.
coredata.dc:description:
In order to alleviate the corrosion of metal in the pickling process, three kinds of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) inhibitors were obtained via the decomposition of natural citric acid derivative under various preparation temperatures (150, 180, 210 °C) for 2 h. Structural characterizations showed that N element was successfully doped into the carbon dots to form pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N. Electrochemistry results implied that the corrosion current density of steel in 200 mg/L of N-CDs solutions was 24.1, 10.7 and 40.4 μA/cm2, which was greatly lower than that in 1 M HCl environment. In this case, the inhibitive efficiency of these N-CDs was higher than 90%. Through calculation, the standard adsorption free energy (ΔG
0
ads ) of as-prepared N-CDs was −25.99, −26.94 and −25.78 kJ/mol, indicating that the adsorption film involved both physisorption and chemisorption. Furthermore, the molecular simulation calculation suggested that the as-prepared N-CDs were parallelly adsorbed on the steel/solution interface. This novel and green corrosion inhibitor was promising to solve the corrosion problem of metal equipment in the acidic process.

head.ce:title: Corrosion inhibition behavior and mechanism of N-doped carbon dots for metal in acid environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In order to alleviate the corrosion of metal in the pickling process, three kinds of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) inhibitors were obtained via the decomposition of natural citric acid derivative under various preparation temperatures (150, 180, 210 °C) for 2 h. Structural characterizations showed that N element was successfully doped into the carbon dots to form pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N. Electrochemistry results implied that the corrosion current density of steel in 200 mg/L of N-CDs solutions was 24.1, 10.7 and 40.4 μA/cm2, which was greatly lower than that in 1 M HCl environment. In this case, the inhibitive efficiency of these N-CDs was higher than 90%. Through calculation, the standard adsorption free energy (ΔG
0
ads ) of as-prepared N-CDs was −25.99, −26.94 and −25.78 kJ/mol, indicating that the adsorption film involved both physisorption and chemisorption. Furthermore, the molecular simulation calculation suggested that the as-prepared N-CDs were parallelly adsorbed on the steel/solution interface. This novel and green corrosion inhibitor was promising to solve the corrosion problem of metal equipment in the acidic process.

ce:section.ce:para: Pickling is one of the necessary measures to clean the inner surface of the heating equipment, which can prevent accidents caused by the corrosion and the scaling of heated surface, and then improve the water/steam quality of the unit (Daoud et al., 2015; Hmamou et al., 2013). To reduce the metal corrosion during pickling process, a corrosion inhibitor should be added into the acid solution to alleviate the corrosion of metal (Alibakhshi et al., 2019; Samiee et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). The selection and application of corrosion inhibitors are an important part during the entire chemical cleaning process. It not only directly affects the chemical cleaning result, but also affects the safety, the service life of equipment and the cost of pickling. In the 21st century, the environmental awareness is enhanced and the national sustainable development strategy has penetrated into the heart of people. In order to achieve the harmony between people and nature, some new requirements are put forward for the development of corrosion inhibitors (Zhang et al., 2015). It is imperative to develop low-toxic and non-polluting corrosion inhibitors to replace some traditional inhibitors such as chromate, phosphate and molybdate (Eletre et al., 2005).

Eco-friendly carbon-based nanomaterial, such as carbon nanotube, carbon fiber, carbon nitride, graphene, graphene quantum dots, has been extensively investigated in various fields because of their unique structures and performances (Cakici et al., 2017; Choi et al., 2019; Hassan et al., 2014; Mishr et al., 2019; Reddy et al., 2008). Due to excellent photochemical properties, good biocompatibility and non-toxicity, carbon dots have attracted a great deal of interest among researchers. Currently, most of the research on carbon dots focuses on fluorescence, catalysis, sampling technique and biomedical imaging (Liu et al., 2017; Dang et al., 2018; You et al., 2018). For example, Sooksin et al. (2018) synthesized the N-CDs by using ferric ion as catalysts, and discovered that the as-obtained N-CDs exhibited a good fluorescent emission. Song et al. (2017) found that the addition of nitrogen in carbon dots could improve the progress of photoluminescence efficiency and tunability of photoluminescence emission. Guo et al. (2017) studied the application of N-CDs in the biological field, and found that this N-CDs displayed special luminescence properties, strong water dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility. Furthermore, N-CDs were also used as a corrosion inhibitor because they owned more eco-friendly, better solubility, richer raw materials, lower cost, and simpler preparation compared with some traditional inhibitors, which could be used to alleviate or solve the corrosion of metal in the acid environment (Phamtruong et al., 2018). For instance, Cui et al. (2017) studied the effect of N-CDs concentration on the electrochemistry behaviors of steel in acid solution, and pointed out that the N-CDs manifested the strongest inhibition effect for steel at 5 mg/L. Then, two N-CDs were further obtained by their team and the weight loss measurements of steel under hydrochloric acid were discussed (Cui et al., 2018). Through analysis, the weight loss of steel in N-CDs solution was greatly decreased than that in blank solution. In the meantime, our previous works developed some N-CDs from citric acid derivatives and studied their corrosion inhibition behaviors on metal in HCl and NaCl solutions (Yang et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2019a, 2020a). It was found that the corrosion inhibition efficiency was very high at low inhibitor concentration, while the inhibition mechanism of these N-CDs was not deeply illuminated by molecular simulation calculation.

In present work, citric acid-based carbon dots under various reaction temperatures were prepared by using pyrolysis method. The main objective was to explore the relationships between reaction temperature, microstructure and corrosion inhibition ability of as-obtained N-CDs in 1 M HCl environment. Meanwhile, the molecular dynamics and quantum chemical simulation calculation were used to give profound insights into the corrosion inhibition mechanism of as-obtained N-CDs.

Ammonium citrate and hydrochloric acid (37%) were obtained from Taitan technology co. LTD (Shanghai, China), the cost comparison of several inhibitors was listed Table S1 (Salarvand et al., 2017; Gutiérrez et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2016; Elhajjaji et al., 2018; Boumhara et al., 2015; Javadian et al., 2017; Dutta et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018; Liao et al., 2017; Qiang et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2020). The Q235 steel as the test electrode was obtained from Xinyu steel company (Xinyu, China). Prior to testing, all electrode were firstly polished with 400–1500 mesh emery papers, and then washed with deionized water and ethanol.

Fig. 1 displayed the schematic procedure of N-CDs and the growth mechanism was shown in previous studies (Dong et al., 2012; Sheng et al., 2011). Firstly, 2 g of ammonium citrate was weighted and then transferred to a flask to heat under various temperatures (150, 180, 210 °C) in an oven for 2 h. After that, these black products were transferred into 60 mL of deionized water and 10 min of ultrasonic treatment was applied. Then, the mixtures were purified by centrifuge, dialysis treatment and PVDF membrane in sequence. Subsequently, the obtained N-CDs were dried in oven at 60 °C for 1 day. Finally, the precision electronic scale was used to measure the quality of N-CDs and then added into 1 M HCl solution to form different test environments (0, 50, 100, 150, 200 mg/L). The N-CDs prepared at 150, 180, 210 °C corresponded to C1, C2 and C3 samples, respectively.

FTIR, UV–Vis, XRD, PL, Raman and XPS were chosen to measure the microstructure and component of inhibitor. SPM and TEM were selected to obtain the morphology, distribution, grain size and height of inhibitor.

Before test, all samples were weighted thrice through precision electronic scale in a constant temperature thermostat. Subsequently, all samples were immerged in various test solutions. After that, the samples were rapidly removed, thoroughly cleaned by ultrasonic in deionized water and ethanol, subsequently dried and weighed. The corrosion rates of all samples were calculated from equation (1) (Ye et al., 2020b, 2020c):
(1)
υ

W 1 − W 2
S ∗ t

CHI-660E electrochemical workstation and correlative equipment were selected to record the electrochemical test. The solution volume used for the electrochemical measurement was 300 mL. Combined with the inhibitor concentration, the mass of N-CDs at 50, 100, 150 and 200 mg/L was 15, 30, 45 and 60 mg, respectively. The classical three-electrode system was chosen for the electrochemical analysis. All electrochemistry measurement should be performed after the OCP was stable. During EIS test, all samples were measured from 105 to 10−2 Hz with 20 mV amplitude. In addition, the Tafel curves were executed at 0.2 mV s−1 within ±250 mV versus OCP.

After corrosion, the sample was cleaned and dried in sequence. S4800 SEM was chosen to detect the 2D surface morphology. LSM700 LSCM was selected for the analysis of 3D surface morphology after immersion. Raman and XPS were used to analyze the component of corrosion products. Among them, the element content of corrosion products was tested three times.

The software of Material Studio 7.0 was selected to the molecular simulation calculation. The density functional theory (DFT) was used to optimize the molecular structure of as-obtained N-CDs. The HOMO, LUMO and quantum chemical parameters (E LUMO, E HOMO, ΔE, E binding) were discussed in detail. The adsorption type and strength of as-obtained N-CDs at the interface were illustrated. During molecular simulation, the system consisted of 10 chloride ions, 6 layers of Fe (110), 10 hydrogen ions, 1 inhibitor molecule and 200 H2O molecules.

The structure characterization results were shown in Fig. 2 . Clearly, many absorption peaks were observed on as-obtained N-CDs (Fig. 2a). By confirmation, some obvious peaks at 1185.1, 1642.8, 1716.9, and 3419.9 cm−1 corresponded to C–OH, CN, CO, and O–H vibration. The weak peaks at 1306.9, 1399.9, 1615.8 and 2999.9 cm−1 belonged to the typical absorption of C–N, COO−, N–H and C–H (Dong et al., 2012). The discovery of C–N and CN absorption peaks implied the successful doping of N atoms. In the case of UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2b), there were two absorption peaks on as-obtained N-CDs. Among them, the one at 345 nm was attributed to the n–π∗ electronic transition of CN bonds. Other peak at 240 nm was corresponded to the π–π∗ electronic transition of sp2 domains (Samanta et al., 2017). From XRD spectra (Fig. 2c), a broad peak of (002) at 25.6° was detected, manifesting the formation of graphite structure in as-obtained N-CDs. For Raman spectra (Fig. 2d), all samples demonstrated two noticeable peaks at 1360 and 1584 cm−1, which was assigned to D and G bands, deriving from the vibration absorption of sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms in graphene skeleton. In the meantime, the ID/IG value can be used to reflect the degree of defect in graphene skeleton (Cancado et al., 2004; Ferrari and Robertson, 2000). The higher the value is, the more defect the graphene skeleton is. By calculation, the ID/IG value of C1, C2 and C3 samples was 1.035, 1.047, and 1.029, respectively. It showed that the doping content of N into the graphene skeleton was the highest in C2 sample, which could disrupt the state of pristine graphene skeleton and increase the surface defect. In PL spectrum, the occurrence of peak at 449 nm illustrated the fluorescence of N-CDs (Fig. 2e). Meanwhile, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen were found on as-obtained N-CDs, these analyses verified the successful doping of nitrogen during preparation (Fig. 2f).

By statistics, Table S2 showed the atomic and weight percentage of element in N-CDs, which was obtained through XPS analysis. Obviously, the percentage of oxygen in N-CDs was stable as the increase of reaction temperature. Nevertheless, an obvious fluctuation was detected on the percentage of carbon and nitrogen elements. In detail, the nitrogen percentage increased at first and then decreased, while the carbon percentage revealed an opposite variation. The above-mentioned variation implied that the positions of partial carbon atoms were replaced through nitrogen atoms. Meanwhile, the change of nitrogen content was consistent with the result of ID/IG. For XPS analysis, four peaks at 284.7, 285.9, 287.1 and 288.1 eV corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/CN, C–N and CO were observed on C 1s spectra (Fig. 3 a) (Li et al., 2012; Ye et al., 2019b). Moreover, all N 1s spectra had a binding energy value of 398.2, 399.5 and 401.1 eV, which was assigned to pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N bonds, respectively (Fig. 3b) (Sheng et al., 2011). Among them, pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N with isolated electron pairs facilitated the formation of coordination bonds at the metal interface (Ye et al., 2020a). Through measurement, Table S3 revealed the specific form of nitrogen element. From the result that the reaction temperature was closely related to the specific form of nitrogen element. By contrast, the sum of pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N in C2 sample was the highest among all samples, which was beneficial to enhance the coordination bond strength between inhibitor and metal interface.

Fig. 4 demonstrated the TEM and SPM results of C1, C2 and C3 samples. Clearly, as the reaction temperature increased, the grain size of N-CDs decreased and then gradually rose. The C1 sample displayed a homogeneous spherical characteristic and the grain size was about 3–6 nm (Fig. 4a). Nevertheless, the grain size of C2 sample was 2–3 nm and these particles were distributed more evenly than C1 sample (Fig. 4b). When the reaction temperature further increased, the C3 sample showed obvious agglomeration and the grain size reached up to 10–15 nm (Fig. 4c). In SPM image, the C1 and C2 samples were granuliform and presented a uniform dispersion. By measurement, the height profile of C1 sample was about of 3–6 nm (Fig. 4d and g). When the reaction temperature increased to 180 °C, the height profile decreased to 1.5–3 nm (Fig. 4e and h). A rebound phenomenon was observed on C3 sample, and the height profile sharply increased to 5–17 nm, which was the highest among all samples (Fig. 4f and i). These phenomena indicated that the reaction temperature could significantly affect the grain size and dispersion of N-CDs.

Fig. 5 exhibited the result of polarization curves. Meantime, Table 1 listed the E

By observation, the E
corr moved toward the positive direction and the i
corr decreased significantly after adding N-CDs, revealing that the inhibitor could prevent anode reaction. At the same time, the i
corr value decreased as the increase of N-CDs content, suggesting that the corrosion rate was alleviated. Moreover, the b
a and b
c decreased as the decrease of i
corr value, indicating that the as-obtained N-CDs belonged to mixed type. Sanaei et al. (2019) pointed out that the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the active anodic sites could reduce the dissolution of iron and then suppress the occurrence of anodic reaction. Thus, the anode got a greater degree of inhibition compared with cathode. According to the previous study, the η value could be used to reflect the inhibition capacity (Yang et al., 2019). By calculation, the η displayed a decreasing trend as the increase of N-CDs content and the max values reached up to 94.23% at 150 °C, 97.43% at 180 °C and 90.32% at 210 °C, which was higher than Rollinia occidentalis extract (85.1% at 1000 ppm) (Salarvand et al., 2017), imidazole derivative (80.9% at 10 mM) (Gutiérrez et al., 2016), Artemisia Mesatlantica essential oil (89.3% at 3 g/L) (Boumhara et al., 2015), bis-benzimidazole derivative (88.0% at 1 mM) (Dutta et al., 2015), melamine derivative (85.3% at 1 mM) (Liao et al., 2017), Glycine max extract (67.1% at 200 ppm) (Javadian et al., 2017), Cuscuta reflexa extract (80.7% at 200 ppm) (Javadian et al., 2017) and Spirogyra extract (86.6% at 200 ppm) (Javadian et al., 2017). It was worth mentioning that the reaction temperature played a critical factor on the inhibition behavior. Under the same condition, the i
corr decreased firstly and then increased as the reaction temperature increased, indicating that suitable reaction temperature could effectively suppress the corrosion. The main reason for the improvement of protection property was the formation of protective film containing N-CDs.

Fig. 6 presented the EIS images and the equivalent circuit model within 24 h immersion. All Nyquist plots revealed one time constant and the shape of arc had no obvious change, indicating that the charge transfer control dominated the interface reaction and the reaction mechanism did not change after adding N-CDs (Zhang et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the size of capacitive loop was amplified after adding N-CDs, revealing that the as-obtained N-CDs could be adsorbed on the steel/solution interface to protect the steel (Qiang et al., 2018). In addition, the size of capacitive loop increased as the increase of N-CDs content, which was because the steel surface was covered by more N-CDs. In other words, the increasing inhibitor enhanced the compactness of adsorption film. Meanwhile, the size of capacitive loop increased firstly and then decreased as the reaction temperature increased. This finding implied that the suitable reaction temperature could greatly enhance the inhibition ability of N-CDs to steel and the dissolution of steel electrode would be accelerated at excessive reaction temperature.

As seen in Bode image, the corrosion resistance of material could be reflected through |Z|
0.01 Hz value (Ye et al., 2018, 2019c, 2020d; Ye et al., 2020d). With the increase of N-CDs content, the |Z|
0.01 Hz revealed an increasing trend. After 24 h immersion, the value increased to 836, 984 and 509 Ω⋅cm2 at 200 mg/L of C1, C2 and C3 environments, which were higher than Artemisia Mesatlantica essential oil (Boumhara et al., 2015), bis-benzimidazole derivative (Dutta et al., 2015), Lemon Balm extract (Asadi et al., 2019), Mangifera indica leaves extract (Ramezanzadeh et al., 2019), Citrullus lanatus fruit extract (Dehghani et al., 2019a), Borage flower extract (Dehghani et al., 2019b) and Chinese gooseberry fruit shell extract (Dehghani et al., 2019c). Meanwhile, the C2 sample displayed the largest |Z|
0.01 Hz value, suggesting the lowest corrosion rate and the highest protective ability. Besides, these phase angle images exhibited one time constant and the peak intensity showed an increasing trend, implying a significant response at steel/solution interface. Fig. S1 revealed the relationship between immersion time and EIS data. As the prolonging of immersion time, the impedance modulus and the size of capacitive loop were enhanced, indicating a stronger inhibition effect. This phenomenon was related to the thickness and compactness of adsorption film. Qiang et al. (2018) pointed out that the thickness and compactness of adsorption film were increased with immersion time, which could replace the corrosion ions on the contact interface and then reduce the occurrence of corrosion.

Table 2 summarized the results of fitting parameters. In circuit model, CPE could more fully describe the actual situation than C

The value of n was inversely proportional to the roughness of steel surface. As seen in Table 2, the roughness of steel surface was decreased with the increase of N-CDs content because the adsorption of N-CDs on the contact interface decreased the surface corrosion (Qiang et al., 2019a). The R
s value in various test environments was stable. Nevertheless, an increasing trend was observed for R
p value by adding inhibitor. Moreover, the increase ranges were amplified as the increase of N-CDs content, suggesting that the steel surface was covered by a mass of N-CDs. Differently, an opposite variation trend was found for C
dl value compared with R
p value, which was due to the fact that the N-CDs gradually replaced the corrosion medium at steel/solution interface. According to the previous study, the C
dl value could be obtained from Helmholtz model (equation (6)) (Sachdev and Gopinath., 2015):
(6)

Because the exposed area of steel was reduced and the electric double-layer thickness was increased after adding N-CDs, resulting in a decrease in C
dl value.

Fig. 7 summarized the weight loss result of specimen under different test conditions. Compared with blank solution, the corrosion rates were greatly declined after adding N-CDs, illustrating the strong protective function (Fig. 7a). In the meantime, the corrosion rate decreased as the increase of inhibitor content, then reached the lowest value at 200 mg/L. For all inhibitors, the corrosion rate was the lowest when steel was immerged into C2 solution, explaining the best protective effect. Moreover, the effect of immersion time was similar with the inhibitor concentration. Obviously, the weight loss under pure HCl environment was aggravated with the extension of immersion time. Nevertheless, an opposite variation was obtained through adding N-CDs, indicating the alleviation of steel corrosion (Fig. 7b).

2D morphology analysis after corrosion test under various environments was exhibited in Fig. 8 . Since the aggressiveness of Cl ion, the steel in blank solution was badly attacked (Fig. 8a). Nevertheless, the attack was obviously relieved after adding N-CDs (Fig.8b ∼ d). By contrast, the smoothest surface was observed in Fig. 8c, implying the slightest corrosion. At the same time, it could be seen form Fig. 8c and e that the corrosion of steel in 100 mg/L solution was more severe than 200 mg/L. This was because N-CDs were easily adsorbed on the interface in high concentration solution. Moreover, the surface became smoother with immersion time (12–24 h), confirming a high-efficiency inhibition function (Fig. 8c and f). According to the element scan analysis, three main elements of iron, carbon as well as nitrogen were observed (Fig.8g ∼ i). Thereinto, nitrogen element was from the as-obtained N-CDs, suggesting that the steel surface was successfully encased through adsorption film.

Fig. 9 presented the 3D morphology analysis after immersion under different test environments. The steel in blank solution revealed severe bulges after immersion 12 h and the Ra was the highest (2.052 μm) among all samples, suggesting the severe corrosion (Fig. 9a). However, the surface became flat and the Ra was sharply reduced after adding as-obtained N-CDs. From Fig.9b ∼ d, the Ra decreased to 1.012, 0.536 and 1.117 μm after immersion into C1, C2 as well as C3 solutions for 12 h, which was 50.68%, 73.88% and 45.57% lower than that in blank solution. It was easy to find that the C2 sample revealed the best corrosion protection effect. At the same time, the Ra value was greatly decreased when the N-CDs content was enhanced (Fig. 9e). Furthermore, the Ra of steel reduced from 0.536 to 0.332 μm as the continued increase of time (12–24 h) (Fig. 9f).

Fig. 10 showed the information of corrosion product obtained from Raman and XPS. It could be seen that a series of characteristic peaks were observed, implying abundant corrosion products (Fig. 10a). Two obvious peaks at 212 and 283 cm−1 were related to α-Fe2O3. Meanwhile, a number of weak peaks were also detected, which located at 399.1, 645.2, 473.6, 554.3 and 628.4 cm−1, corresponding to γ-FeOOH, α-FeOOH and Fe3O4, respectively (Ye et al., 2019d). By contrast, the intensity of these peaks was obviously alleviated after adding N-CDs and the C2 sample presented the strongest inhibition degree. Furthermore, the formation of corrosion product could also be inhibited by changing N-CDs concentration and immersion time. Similar results were found in XPS analysis. Oxygen and chlorine contents were reduced after adding N-CDs, demonstrating the suppression of interface reaction (Fig. 10b).

All adsorption isotherms were calculated from equation (7) (Qiang et al., 2020):

ads ≥ −20 kJ/mol, the essence belonged to physical interaction. If the ΔG
0
ads ≤ −40 kJ/mol, the essence belonged to chemical interaction (Hu et al., 2016). These analysis indicated that the interaction at solution/steel interface was physicochemical adsorption. Nitrogen atom in aromatic heterocyclic ring was the main reason to form the chemical interaction, which could induce the formation of strong coordination bond at solution/steel interface. Meanwhile, the physical interaction was from the graphitic N atom, which could adhere on the surface of steel by agglomeration effect, reducing the exposed region. As indicated above, the C2 inhibitor displayed the most ideal corrosion inhibition effect.

Fig. 12 showed the frontier orbital distribution of as-obtained N-CDs. Clearly, the HOMO covered the entire molecular skeleton, while the LUMO concentrated on partial molecular skeleton (i.e., nitrogen-containing heteroatom), indicating that the active site covered the entire molecule. This finding demonstrated that the N-CDs molecule would be adsorbed on the interface in a parallel state, which could provide maximum protection to the steel surface. Molecular reactivity of compounds is generally studied by analyzing quantum chemical parameters. It is well known that E LUMO represents the electron-accepting ability of inhibitor molecules, and low E LUMO values indicate that molecules are more inclined to accept electrons (Musa et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2011). On the other hand, a high E HOMO value means that the molecule has a strong electron-donating ability. Therefore, the energy gap can reflect the chemical stability of inhibitor molecules. The low ΔE value implies that the inhibitor molecules are easier adsorbed on the metal surface, resulting in a high corrosion inhibition efficiency. Through calculation, the E LUMO, E HOMO and ΔE values of as-obtained N-CDs were about −0.0843, −0.1462, and 0.0619 eV, respectively. Compared with bis-benzimidazole derivative (Dutta et al., 2015), naphthyridine derivative (Singh et al., 2016), Ginkgo leaf extract (Sanaei et al., 2019), allyl imidazolium-based ionic liquids (Dehghani et al., 2019c), 5-nitroindazole (Qiang et al., 2015), Germacrene (Asadi et al., 2019) and Liquritigenin (Alibakhshi et al., 2018), the as-prepared N-CDs were easier to adsorb on the interface.

Fig. 13 showed the equilibrium adsorption configuration of as-obtained N-CDs on Fe (110) substrate. Obviously, the N-CDs inhibitor molecule was closely adsorbed on steel surface in the parallel orientation. Combined with the distribution of frontier molecular orbitals, the adsorption of N-CDs molecule and the formation of coordination bonds were synchronously operated. At this point, the coordination bond could be transferred to the unfilled empty orbital of Fe by the electrons in the HOMO of inhibitor molecule, and the electrons were transferred from the 3d orbital of Fe to the empty orbital of N-CDs inhibitor molecule. Meanwhile, the parallel adsorption mode of N-CDs could fully cover the metal surface and then avoid the contact from corrosive media. In addition, the E binding between the adsorption molecules and the metal surface could reflect the bonding strength of inhibitor molecules. The higher the E binding value is, the stronger the adsorption strength is. By calculation, the E binding value of as-obtained N-CDs was 188.47 kJ⋅mol−1, which was higher than 5-Benzyl-1H-Tetrazole (Qiang et al., 2020), 5-(Benzylthio)-1H-tetrazole (Qiang et al., 2020), 5-nitroindazole (Sigircik et al., 2016), adenine nucleotide (Qiang et al., 2019a), Glabridin (Alibakhshi et al., 2018), ascorbic acid (Sanaei et al., 2019), Nicotinic acid (Dehghani et al., 2019b) and Gallic acid (Ramezanzadeh et al., 2019) in previous reports. Thus, the as-obtained N-CDs displayed an excellent corrosion inhibition ability.

In this work, three kinds of ammonium citrate derived carbon dots under various reaction temperatures (150, 180, 210 °C) were prepared and analyzed. The result revealed that N element was successfully doped into the carbon dots in the form of pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N. By electrochemical analysis, the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs prepared at 150, 180 and 210 °C was 94.23%, 97.43% and 90.32% at 200 mg/L, respectively. Combined with EIS and Tafel results, the N-CDs prepared at 180 °C presented the strongest inhibition effect, which was also verified by corrosion morphologies. In the meantime, the essence of adsorption film was physicochemical interaction, which obeyed the Langmuir adsorption model and was verified by K
ads and ΔG
0
ads values. Furthermore, the molecular dynamics and quantum chemical simulation calculation showed that the N-CDs were powerfully adsorbed at steel/solution interface in a parallel way.
Zhixiong Liu: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Y.W. Ye: Formal analysis, Data curation. H. Chen: Funding acquisition.
